extra.reasoning:
The property requires that no input makes both rec_drug_a and rec_drug_b true simultaneously.

rec_drug_a can only become true via rule rec_a_from_conjunction (priority 50): IF (polypharmacy AND ckd_dialysis AND seizure_history AND low_ejection_fraction) THEN rec_drug_a := true. Otherwise it defaults to false. So rec_drug_a = true requires all four of those inputs true, which includes polypharmacy = true.

rec_drug_b can only become true via rule rec_b_from_trigger (priority 50): IF polypharmacy THEN rec_drug_b := true. Otherwise it defaults to false. So rec_drug_b = true requires polypharmacy = true.

The condition for rec_drug_a (which requires polypharmacy among its conjuncts) is fully compatible with the condition for rec_drug_b (which requires polypharmacy). The two outputs are governed by independent rules with no cross-output suppression — each is the sole non-default rule for its own output, so priority/tie-breaking is irrelevant; both fire whenever their guards hold.

Counterexample input: polypharmacy=true, ckd_dialysis=true, seizure_history=true, low_ejection_fraction=true (prior_gi_bleed and qt_prolongation arbitrary; set false). Under this input:
- rec_a_from_conjunction fires → rec_drug_a := true
- rec_b_from_trigger fires → rec_drug_b := true

Both rec_drug_a and rec_drug_b are true, so the mutual-exclusion property (NOT(rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b)) is violated.
